openingMessage: We win in this business by being invited back the next day. That doesn't just come from doing the job right — it comes from how we carry ourselves as professionals and as people.
leadershipThought: Protect the group. Protect the work. Our jobs, our clients, and our reputation depend on it. If you show up and communicate — you protect the group. If you don't — you expose it.
opsContent:

Carry-Forward:
• Gipple 124H benzene at 10,000 ppm — ongoing; monitor next shift

lessonsLearned:
• Master valve installed wrong direction — Marcos Morales (10:59 AM): pump truck called to kill well before flipping valve; maintenance crew to continue assembly afterward. Active/in-progress at time of recording.
• Needle valves worn out / bleeding — Marcos Morales (post-1st job, ~8 AM area): after completing 1st job, doubled back because smell wasn't dissipating. Found a couple of needle valves worn out and still bleeding. Chris (operator contact) notified; bringing plugs to fix. Marcos headed to 2nd location.
• Leak reported by pumper — Michael Jobe (6:13 AM): pumper flagged a leak at location 202; team finishing 202 then fixing leak before moving to next job.
• SM #152 down — Michael Hutt (6:13 AM). No resolution noted in frames.
• H&P 264 release — Freddy V released till further notice (5:13 PM). No context given.
exxonMoments:
SWA Before Elevation — Unsafe Man Basket Stopped (Line of Fire)
A Canon technician at University 10-11/12H observed unsafe man basket operations and exercised Stop Work Authority before anyone was elevated. The location was left clean and safe after the situation was resolved. Man basket work is one of the highest-risk lift operations on an oil and gas site — SWA before elevation is always the right call when anything about the setup looks wrong.

Exclusion Zone Breach Intercepted — Third-Party Personnel (Line of Fire)
At Big Lake, a workover rig crew and third-party personnel attempted to enter established exclusion zones multiple times during a disconnect. The MT and roustabout crew actively intercepted unauthorized individuals and redirected them safely around the work area. When SimOps create unplanned traffic near your exclusion zone, proactive interception by all crew members — not just the safety tech — is what prevents a line-of-fire exposure.

Iron on the Ground, Not in the Air (Line of Fire)
At Guns Up O 32 29 #4215H, the crew laid iron on the ground instead of making changes to it while suspended. Job completed without incident. Keeping personnel out from under suspended loads is one of the most direct line-of-fire controls available — when you can set it down, set it down.

Spotter Lost Visual — Equipment Operator Called the Stop (Line of Fire)
A Sky Trax operator self-initiated a work stop the moment he lost visual contact with his spotter during operations. The crew identified better attention to wind direction as an area to improve. Equipment operators own Stop Work Authority — stop the moment you lose your spotter; regaining visual contact is the only acceptable restart condition.

Exclusion Zones Held — No Unauthorized Personnel On Pad (Line of Fire)
At TRD 006, the MT verified energy isolation while crane and manlift operated safely with exclusion zones effective throughout. No unauthorized personnel came onsite. Verified energy isolation combined with enforced exclusion zones is the complete line-of-fire control set — one without the other leaves a gap.

fieldNews.headline: Louie Zarate → Justin Pack
fieldNews.detail: Kudos for "Great Attitude" — *"For getting along with everyone! Has a positive attitude every day and never lets anything bring him down! Keep up the great attitude Justin!"*

fieldNews.detail: Marcos Morales (10:59 AM): pump truck called to kill well before flipping valve; maintenance crew to continue assembly afterward. Active/in-progress at time of recording.

fieldNews.detail: Marcos Morales (post-1st job, ~8 AM area): after completing 1st job, doubled back because smell wasn't dissipating. Found a couple of needle valves worn out and still bleeding. Chris (operator contact) notified; bringing plugs to fix. Marcos headed to 2nd location.

fieldNews.detail: Michael Jobe (6:13 AM): pumper flagged a leak at location 202; team finishing 202 then fixing leak before moving to next job.

todaysChallenge: Find one person today and communicate something early — before they need it.
todaysChallengeSubtext: Call leadership. Notify dispatch. Post in Breakroom. No surprises. That is how trust is built.

compliance.footerNote: Update numbers in daily.json each Monday morning.
